behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the tool receives git's rename detection but miscategorizes it as add+delete. To catch this, we need git's actual rename detection working correctly. Git uses heuristics for rename detection (similarity thresholds, content-based matching) that mocks might not accurately represent. Integration testing with a real repo ensures the tool handles actual git rename behavior, not just theoretical formats.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with an initial committed file. Rename the file (keeping content identical) in a new commit. Run the tool. Verify the renamed file appears only in the 'renamed' category and does not appear in 'added' or 'deleted' categories.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires a real git repo and tool execution; Integration level confirmed.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: tool crashes when encountering binary files or counts them incorrectly. Git's output for binary files (e.g., 'Binary files differ') is distinct from text diffs. Mocking this format might miss edge cases in how real git handles specific binary formats (images, executables, PDFs). Integration testing with actual binary files ensures the tool correctly parses and counts real binary diffs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo, add a binary file (e.g., PNG image or compiled executable) in one commit and modify it in another. Run the tool. Verify it does not crash and correctly counts the binary file in the appropriate change categories (added, modified).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real binary files in a git repo; Integration level appropriate.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: tool crashes or errors when run on a freshly initialized repo with no commits. While this edge case is simple enough that Unit testing (mocking empty commit history) could theoretically work, the behavior explicitly states 'running the tool on a repository', implying actual repo execution. Integration ensures the tool handles the true edge case state, including any unexpected git interactions specific to empty repos.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repository (git init) with no commits. Run the tool. Verify it produces an empty report (either empty categories or zero counts) and exits without errors or exceptions.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires initializing a real git repo; Integration level is appropriate (though Unit with mocking would be lower-level alternative).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires testing against 'actual change history'. This is fundamentally an integration concern: verifying the tool's parsing and counting logic correctly aggregates real git changes across multiple scenarios (adds, deletes, renames, modifications). Unit testing with mocked git output would not verify correctness against actual git history semantics and edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with a controlled set of changes: commit 1 adds 5 specific files, commit 2 deletes 3 files, commit 3 renames 2 files (keeping content identical), commit 4 modifies 4 existing files. Run the tool. Verify the output report shows exactly: 5 additions, 3 deletions, 2 renames, 4 modifications.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires creating a repo with known changes and verifying exact counts; Integration level confirmed.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: tool produces different outputs depending on whether it analyzes staged vs unstaged changes. This requires setting up and comparing two distinct repo states—one with staged changes, one with unstaged changes. The tool's behavior must be consistent across these scenarios. This is inherently an integration concern because you must coordinate multiple git operations (staging, creating, committing) to set up the test scenarios.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repo with an initial committed baseline. Scenario A: stage some file changes (add, delete, modify) without committing. Scenario B: make identical changes in the working directory but leave them unstaged (or use a separate repo state). Run the tool in both scenarios. Verify the outputs are identical—the tool either consistently includes or excludes the uncommitted changes.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires setting up staged vs unstaged states and comparing outputs; Integration level required.
